parentQuestion: Which of the following is the major positive ion outside cells?

scaffolds.explain: A CATION is a POSITIVELY charged ion — it has LOST electrons. Memory trick: CATions are PAWSitive.

wrongExplains.0: Negatively charged ions are anions, not cations.
wrongExplains.2: Neutral atoms have no charge; cations are positive.
wrongExplains.3: Cations are charged particles, not molecules.

scaffolds.explain: An ANION is a NEGATIVELY charged ion — it has GAINED electrons. Anions are attracted to the positive electrode (anode).

wrongExplains.0: Positively charged ions are cations, not anions.
wrongExplains.2: Neutral atoms have no charge; anions are negative.
wrongExplains.3: Anions are charged particles, not proteins.

scaffolds.explain: EXTRACELLULAR FLUID is the fluid OUTSIDE cells — includes blood plasma, interstitial fluid, and cerebrospinal fluid.

wrongExplains.0: Intracellular fluid is inside cells; extracellular is outside.
wrongExplains.2: Nuclear fluid is inside cells; ECF is outside cells.
wrongExplains.3: ECF is all fluid outside cells, not just specific organelles.

scaffolds.explain: INTRACELLULAR FLUID is the fluid INSIDE cells, also called cytoplasm. It contains different ion concentrations than ECF.

wrongExplains.0: Extracellular fluid is outside cells; ICF is inside.
wrongExplains.2: Blood plasma is extracellular; ICF is inside cells.
wrongExplains.3: Lymph is extracellular; ICF is inside cells.

scaffolds.q: Which ion has the highest concentration in extracellular fluid?
scaffolds.explain: SODIUM (Na⁺) has the highest concentration in extracellular fluid — about 140 mEq/L outside vs only 10-15 mEq/L inside cells.

wrongExplains.0: Potassium is high INSIDE cells, not outside.
wrongExplains.2: Calcium is present but not the major extracellular cation.
wrongExplains.3: Magnesium is present but not the major extracellular cation.

scaffolds.q: Which ion is the major cation INSIDE cells?
scaffolds.explain: POTASSIUM (K⁺) is the major cation INSIDE cells — about 140 mEq/L inside vs only 4-5 mEq/L outside.

wrongExplains.0: Sodium is high OUTSIDE cells, not inside.
wrongExplains.2: Chloride is an anion, not a cation.
wrongExplains.3: Bicarbonate is an anion, not a cation.

scaffolds.q: Why do cells maintain different ion concentrations inside vs outside?
options: The ion gradients power electrical signaling and transport
scaffolds.explain: Ion GRADIENTS power ELECTRICAL SIGNALING and TRANSPORT. The Na⁺/K⁺ difference creates the membrane potential essential for nerve/muscle function.

wrongExplains.0: Ion distribution is carefully maintained, not random.
wrongExplains.2: Gradients power function, not just storage.
wrongExplains.3: Gradients power signaling, not temperature regulation.

scaffolds.explain: The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPASE PUMP maintains the gradient by pumping 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in, using ATP energy.

wrongExplains.0: Gravity doesn't affect ion distribution in cells.
wrongExplains.2: Diffusion would equalize gradients; the pump maintains them.
wrongExplains.3: Body heat doesn't pump ions against their gradient.

scaffolds.q: Which of the following is the major positive ion outside cells?
scaffolds.explain: SODIUM (Na⁺) is the major positive ion (cation) OUTSIDE cells. Potassium is the major cation inside cells.

wrongExplains.0: Potassium is the major cation INSIDE cells, not outside.
wrongExplains.2: Calcium is present but not the major extracellular cation.
wrongExplains.3: H⁺ is present but not the major extracellular cation.
